lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 812199 providing repeat external-customer personal-care services, including nonmedical hair-removal, tattoo and piercing, permanent-makeup, tanning, sauna or steam-bath, hair-replacement, and related day-spa services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hair, nail, facial, and nonmedical diet or weight-reducing services assigned to other industries; medical or surgical services; self-service-only machines; shells, captive internal units, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximate $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily consumer-paid appointments, sessions, packages, memberships, and product add-ons; revenue is local, transaction-heavy, and often driven by repeat visits, referrals, practitioner reputation, and capacity utilization.

a.rationale: Core delivery is embodied work on a customer, so the exposed share is concentrated in intake, scheduling, reminders, consent and aftercare communications, marketing, bookkeeping, inventory, and portions of consultation rather than treatment itself. BLS task descriptions for the overlapping skincare workforce show both physical procedures and advisory work.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from a representative sample of tattoo, piercing, hair-removal, tanning, and day-spa operators, with current software automation separately identified.
caveats: No source measures wage-weighted AI task exposure for this six-digit industry.
The BLS skincare occupation overlaps only part of the heterogeneous NAICS population.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio may blend materially different labor models, including employee, booth, and contractor arrangements.

rho.rationale: The exposed work is mostly common small-business workflow that can be implemented through booking, CRM, communications, content, and back-office tools within five years. Adoption will be reduced by fragmentation, practitioner-led workflows, uneven data quality, consent and privacy requirements, safety-critical customer communications, and limited change capacity in small locations.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor cohort data showing five-year adoption, active use, labor-hours removed, error rates, and retention for AI-enabled booking, CRM, intake, and back-office tools among NAICS 812199 operators.
caveats: Implementation evidence is not reported at six-digit NAICS level.
Practitioner acceptance and local licensing or consent rules can vary by service and state.

e.rationale: Most included services are sold repeatedly to external customers, but eligibility is reduced by solo practices dependent on one artist or practitioner, contractor-heavy shops, small owner-operated establishments below the band, and business-model differences across the code. The supplied firm count is already restricted to the LMM band, so the range focuses on service-model eligibility and transferability rather than size.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or transaction database classifying LMM operators by service line, external-customer revenue, owner dependence, contractor mix, and normalized EBITDA.
caveats: The industry is unusually heterogeneous and no source enumerates the eligible LMM subset.
BLS reports substantial self-employment in the overlapping skincare occupation, but occupation self-employment is not a direct firm-eligibility measure.
The supplied firm count is an estimate derived using a broad-sector margin bridge.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sell, transfer, or public-offering plans among US business owners: 22% for employer-business owners and 9% for nonemployers. The base discounts the employer rate for plans that do not complete or do not create a qualifying control transfer and for practitioner-dependent personal-care firms; the resulting range likely still overstates completed transfers if stated intentions are optimistic, while exclusion of unsolicited sales creates possible downward error.
s5.rationale: The broad owner survey provides a bounded intention benchmark, but personal-care firms often depend on the owner's labor or reputation and may close rather than transfer. A base below the employer-owner intention rate reflects completion risk and transferability constraints.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators for NAICS 812199 from a business-broker, SBA loan, or private transaction database over rolling five-year cohorts.
caveats: Gallup covers all US business industries rather than NAICS 812199.
The survey measures owner intentions, not completed qualifying control transfers.
Employer and nonemployer findings do not isolate firms in the supplied LMM band.

q.rationale: Consumer-paid per-session, package, and membership pricing offers no formal cost-plus pass-through, so an operator can retain part of an implemented benefit. Retention is moderated by transparent local pricing, discounts, low switching costs in some services, practitioner compensation claims, and competitive reinvestment in marketing or customer experience.
q.change_evidence: Operator-level panel data linking realized labor savings to price changes, practitioner compensation, promotion spend, churn, and contribution margin for comparable locations over several renewal or repricing cycles.
caveats: No source directly measures benefit sharing or five-year retention for this industry.
Pricing power differs sharply by service, local density, practitioner reputation, and membership penetration.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in the broader NAICS 812100 personal-care-services industry to rise 8.9% from 2024 to 2034, and Pew finds a large installed US tattoo population. The base converts the broader ten-year employment direction into a more conservative five-year constant-price demand ratio for the mixed 812199 service basket. The broader category includes hair, nail, and skincare services and employment can diverge from demand, so it may overstate or understate this code; tattoo prevalence establishes demand depth but not growth and therefore only supports the lower-tail resistance to collapse.
d5.rationale: Repeat discretionary personal-care demand should be broadly stable with modest growth, supported by category employment projections and a substantial tattoo customer base. Cyclicality, saturation, service substitution, and mix differences warrant a range spanning mild contraction to meaningful growth.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year revenue or quantity indexes for NAICS 812199 by service line, ideally from Census Service Annual Survey detail, payment-card panels, or operator same-store visit data.
caveats: The BLS forecast is for broader NAICS 812100 and a ten-year employment outcome, not five-year constant-quality demand.
Pew tattoo prevalence is a 2023 population level rather than an industry growth series.
The current basket mixes services with different cyclicality, saturation, and technology substitution.

o.bridge: Census industry examples and BLS skincare tasks describe physical services performed on a customer's body, such as hair removal, tattooing or piercing-related personal care, skin evaluation, and equipment sanitation. These task descriptions are transformed into the share of year-five service quantity needing an accountable operator: most current-basket quantity remains operator-delivered, with a discount for home devices, self-service tanning or sauna formats, and software-supported self-care. Because the occupation source covers skincare rather than every 812199 service, the mapping may overstate operator need where self-service equipment is common and understate it where licensed physical work dominates.
o.rationale: The current service basket is predominantly embodied, safety-sensitive, and delivered at a location by a practitioner or accountable facility. Software can change discovery, booking, consultation, and follow-up, but generally cannot eliminate the operator responsible for the physical procedure.
o.change_evidence: Service-line quantity data tracking migration to home hair-removal devices, self-service formats, remote or automated delivery, and practitioner-free alternatives among current customers.
caveats: Operator requirement varies substantially across tattoo, piercing, hair-removal, tanning, sauna, and related services.
The BLS task source is an overlapping occupation proxy rather than a complete industry workflow census.

narrative.executive_view: Other personal-care services combine a meaningful but bounded back-office AI opportunity with service delivery that remains highly physical and local. The recurring customer relationship is attractive operationally, but heterogeneous service lines, practitioner dependence, and uncertain LMM transfer volume limit confidence.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can reduce avoidable labor in lead response, scheduling, reminders, intake drafting, routine customer communications, marketing content, bookkeeping, inventory, and management reporting. It is much less able to substitute for the physical procedure, sanitation, safety judgment, and trust-building work at the point of service.
narrative.value_capture: Direct consumer pricing permits operators to keep some productivity gain rather than contractually passing it through. Local price visibility, promotion intensity, practitioner economics, and low switching costs in some categories will redirect part of the gain to lower prices, marketing, wages, or improved service.
narrative.firm_availability: The code plausibly contains repeat-service external-customer firms in the LMM band, but many establishments are too small, contractor-heavy, or tied to an individual practitioner's reputation. Broad owner-intention data indicate a transfer pool, yet completed industry-specific control-transfer evidence is missing.
narrative.demand_durability: The service basket should remain mostly operator-required because treatment is performed on the customer. Broader category projections indicate modest growth, while tattoo prevalence supports a durable installed customer base; discretionary spending, saturation, home devices, and self-service formats create downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal research risk is aggregation: tattoo, piercing, tanning, hair-removal, sauna, and related services have different labor models, regulation, cyclicality, and self-service threats. Key operating risks include licensing and consent requirements, health and safety incidents, practitioner retention, reputation concentration, local competition, and dependence on discretionary consumer demand.
narrative.principal_driver: A high share of current-basket quantity still requires local physical delivery, while common administrative workflows offer implementable AI savings.
narrative.principal_weakness: The six-digit code is heterogeneous and practitioner-dependent, leaving firm eligibility and qualifying transfer rates weakly evidenced.

sources.title: NAICS Sector 81: Other Services (except Public Administration)
sources.supports: Official industry definition and examples for NAICS 812199, used for the lens and the physical-service/operator-required assessment.

sources.title: Skincare Specialists - Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Overlapping occupation task mix, work setting, licensing, employment, and self-employment evidence used directionally for AI exposure, implementation, eligibility, and operator requirement.

sources.title: Industry Employment and Output Projections
sources.supports: Broader NAICS 812100 employment projection from 2024 to 2034 used as a directional demand proxy.

sources.title: 32% of Americans have a tattoo, including 22% who have more than one
sources.supports: National 2023 tattoo prevalence and survey methodology, used as a level indicator of one included service line's customer base.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall-2024 US business-owner survey reporting five-year sell or transfer intentions for employer and nonemployer owners, used as the control-transfer proxy.
